title: 26 — Sin
html:
The Big Question

Why do we do what we know is wrong? What drives us, again and again, to harm what we love most?

He was sixteen. It was an ordinary Sunday night in Thagaste, a small town in the Roman province of Numidia, North Africa. Augustine and his friends had been drinking. They walked past a pear tree laden with fruit. None of them was hungry. It was not a particularly beautiful tree. The fruit was not even ripe. But something drove them: they shook the tree, tore off the pears, and threw them to the pigs. Just like that. Pure destruction, with no benefit whatsoever.

Thirty years later, already bishop of Hippo and one of the greatest saints and theologians of the Church, Augustine devoted entire pages of his Confessions to examining that absurd night. «What was it that I loved in that theft?» he asked himself. And the answer he found still shakes us: «I loved nothing other than the theft itself... I sought in the theft a counterfeit of omnipotence.» He did not steal out of need. He stole because evil itself attracted him.

Augustine grasped something the Catechism of the Catholic Church teaches with crystal clarity: sin is, above all, a mystery. It is not simply «breaking a rule.» It is an offense against reason, truth, and right conscience; it is a failure in genuine love for God and neighbor (CCC 1849). And yet, however much we analyze it, a core of absurdity remains: how is it possible that, knowing the good, we choose evil?

This lesson addresses one of the most serious topics in the Catechism. But it is not a lesson of condemnation. It is a lesson of truth and hope. Because if we do not understand what sin is, neither will we understand what mercy is. And God's mercy — as we will see at the end — is greater than any sin: misericordia Dei maior omni peccato.

Activity

Think of a time when you did something knowing it was wrong. It does not have to be something very serious. Maybe it was a small lie, a hurtful word, something you took without permission. Do you remember what you felt before, during, and after? Augustine said that sin promises freedom but delivers slavery. Do you recognize that pattern in your experience?

Write on a piece of paper, anonymously, one word that describes how you felt after doing something wrong. The catechist can collect them and read them aloud without identifying anyone. The goal is not to shame, but to recognize that we all share this human experience.

1. What Is Sin? The Church's Definition

The Catechism devotes more than thirty paragraphs to sin (CCC 1846-1876), and the first thing it offers is a definition that deserves to be read slowly:

«Sin is an offense against reason, truth, and right conscience; it is failure in genuine love for God and neighbor caused by a perverse attachment to certain goods. It wounds the nature of man and injures human solidarity.» (CCC 1849)

Let us examine each part:

«Offense against reason»: Sin is not merely a legal infraction; it is an irrational act. When we sin, our intelligence is darkened, it judges goods wrongly, it sets as its ultimate end something that is merely a means. St. Thomas Aquinas teaches that every sin includes an error of practical reason: we confuse an apparent good with the true good.

«Offense against truth»: Every sin rests on a lie. The serpent in Genesis begins by saying: «Did God really say...?» (Gen 3:1). The tempter always twists the truth. Sin makes us live in falsehood: about God, about ourselves, about others.

«Offense against right conscience»: Conscience is the interior voice that calls us to do good and avoid evil (cf. lesson 24). To sin is to ignore that voice, to silence it, or to deform it progressively. A badly formed conscience ends up calling good evil and evil good (Is 5:20).

«Failure in genuine love for God and neighbor»: Here is the heart of the definition. Sin is not primarily a violation of norms, but a rupture of relationships. It is an act of un-love. That is why the greatest commandment is love (Mt 22:37-40), and every sin is, at its root, a negation of love.

«Caused by a perverse attachment to certain goods»: No one chooses evil as evil. We always choose something that presents itself as a good: pleasure, power, comfort, security. The problem is that we sacrifice a greater good (God, friendship, integrity) for a lesser good (an immediate pleasure). The perversion lies in the disorder of our loves.

«Wounds the nature of man and injures human solidarity»: Sin is never a private matter. It harms the one who commits it (making him less human, less free, less capable of love) and harms the entire human community. Every sin weakens the fabric of solidarity.

St. Augustine's Definition

The Catechism also cites the classic definition of St. Augustine, which complements the one above:

«Sin is an utterance, a deed, or a desire contrary to the eternal law.» (CCC 1849, citing St. Augustine, Contra Faustum 22,27)

The «eternal law» is the divine reason that governs the entire universe. It is not an arbitrary law imposed by a capricious legislator; it is the very wisdom of God, which shows us how to live according to our nature. To sin is to go against that wisdom. It is like trying to use a phone underwater or a fish out of water: it goes against the nature of the thing and ends in destruction.

The Three Dimensions of Sin

Christian tradition distinguishes three directions in which sin breaks communion:

  • Against God: Sin is, above all, an offense against God. «Against you, you alone, have I sinned,» cries David in Psalm 51. Even though the harm may affect our neighbor, the deepest dimension of sin is theological: we reject God's love and disobey his law.
  • Against neighbor: Sin harms others: theft deprives someone of what is theirs, calumny destroys reputation, violence wounds or kills, scandal leads others into evil. Every sin has a social dimension.
  • Against oneself: Sin wounds one's own dignity. It makes us less free, less human, less capable of loving. The sinner self-destructs. As St. Paul says: «The wages of sin is death» (Rom 6:23).

Context

In our culture, we speak little of sin and much of «error,» «mistake,» or «miscalculation.» But reducing sin to a technical error is to deny human freedom and the gravity of moral evil. If I merely «make a mistake,» it is enough to correct the algorithm. If I sin, I need forgiveness. The difference is enormous.

Pope Benedict XVI warned against the «loss of the sense of sin» as one of the greatest tragedies of our time. When awareness of sin disappears, so does the need for salvation. And without the need for salvation, Jesus Christ becomes irrelevant. That is why this lesson is so important: recovering the sense of sin is recovering the sense of redemption.

2. Original Sin and Its Consequences

We cannot understand actual sin without going back to the origin. The Catechism treats original sin at length in paragraphs 385-421 (which correspond to the Creed lesson on the Fall). Here it suffices to recall the essentials for our topic.

The Genesis account (Gen 3:1-19) describes, in symbolic language but with profound theological truth, the first sin of humanity. Adam and Eve, created in friendship with God, in harmony with themselves and with creation, are tempted by the serpent. The temptation presents itself as an invitation to be «like gods,» knowing good and evil (Gen 3:5). That is: to decide for themselves what is right and what is wrong, without reference to God. To take God's place.

This is the core of every sin: the claim to absolute autonomy. Not wanting to depend on God. Wanting to be the measure of all things. The original pride.

The consequences of original sin are universal:

  • Loss of original grace: Humanity loses the holiness and justice in which it was created.
  • The wounding of human nature: Although not totally corrupted (as Luther and Calvin erroneously taught), it is weakened in its natural powers, subject to ignorance, suffering, and the dominion of death. Above all, it is inclined to sin: this inclination is called concupiscence.
  • Transmission to all human beings: Original sin is transmitted by propagation, not by imitation. We are all born with this wound, deprived of grace. That is why Baptism is necessary: it frees us from original sin and restores the life of grace.

The good news is that, where sin abounded, grace abounded all the more (Rom 5:20). Original sin is not the final word. The final word is Christ, the new Adam, whose obedience repairs the disobedience of the first.

Connection

Original sin explains why doing good is hard and doing evil comes easily. It is not an excuse: we remain free and responsible. But it is a diagnosis: our nature is wounded. That is why we need grace. Trying to overcome sin by willpower alone is like trying to cure a serious illness with good intentions. We need the Physician.

It also explains the universal experience of evil. Why is there suffering? Why is human history full of wars, injustices, betrayals? Original sin is the Christian answer to the problem of evil: not all evil comes from current personal decisions, but from an original wound that affects us all and whose consequences we live every day.

For reflection: In what areas of your life do you most notice the inclination to evil? Where is it hardest for you to do good?

3. The Gravity of Sin: Mortal and Venial

Not all sins are equal. Scripture and Tradition distinguish between mortal sin and venial sin. This distinction is not a theological technicality; it is vital for the spiritual life.

Mortal Sin

Mortal sin destroys charity in the heart of man by a grave violation of God's law. It turns man away from God, who is his ultimate end and his beatitude, preferring an inferior good (CCC 1855).

«For a sin to be mortal, three conditions must together be met: ‘Mortal sin is sin whose object is grave matter and which is also committed with full knowledge and deliberate consent.’» (CCC 1857)

Let us examine these three conditions, which are essential for an examination of conscience and for Confession:

1. Grave matter: The act itself must be objectively grave. The Ten Commandments specify grave matters: homicide, adultery, grave theft, false witness, idolatry... Not everything constitutes grave matter. The Church, in her wisdom, helps us discern what is grave and what is not.

2. Full knowledge: I must know that what I am doing is gravely wrong. A vague intuition is not enough; a clear knowledge of the malice of the act is required. Ignorance can diminish or even nullify imputability (CCC 1860). That is why formation of conscience is so important: someone who does not know that something is a sin may not be fully responsible.

3. Deliberate consent: I must freely choose to do it. A decision coerced by force, an addiction that diminishes freedom, an impulsive emotional movement that gives no time for reflection... can all reduce responsibility. Mortal sin requires a fully voluntary act.

The consequences of mortal sin are grave: it deprives one of sanctifying grace and, if unrepented, leads to eternal death (the definitive separation from God, which we call hell). To be forgiven, it requires the sacrament of Penance (Confession) or, at least, an act of perfect contrition together with the firm purpose of going to confession.

Venial Sin

Venial sin does not destroy charity, but it wounds and weakens it:

«Venial sin is committed when, in a less serious matter, one does not observe the measure prescribed by the moral law, or when one disobeys the moral law in a grave matter but without full knowledge or without complete consent.» (CCC 1862)

Venial sin does not break the covenant with God, but it cools it. It does not deprive one of grace, but it disposes one toward mortal sin. It is like small cracks in a wall: each one does not bring the wall down, but many, over time, weaken it until it collapses.

The Catechism teaches that «deliberate and unrepented venial sin disposes us little by little to commit mortal sin» (CCC 1863). We cannot take venial sins lightly. They are like a constant low fever: it does not kill instantly, but it drains one's strength. The struggle against venial sin is an essential part of the Christian life.

Further Study

The distinction between mortal and venial sin has been denied by some Protestant groups that maintain that every sin deserves eternal condemnation. The Catholic Church, however, follows Scripture: «All wrongdoing is sin, but there is sin that is not mortal» (1 Jn 5:17). Not all sins break communion with God in the same way.

St. Thomas Aquinas explains that the difference between mortal and venial sin is a difference of genus: mortal sin turns one away from the ultimate end (God), while venial sin only involves a disorder in the means without turning one away from the end. It is the difference between taking the wrong road (mortal) and stumbling on the right road (venial).

In pastoral practice, discerning between grave and light matter requires prudence. The Church offers objective criteria (the commandments, moral doctrine), but each case must be examined with the help of a confessor, who acts as physician and judge of souls.

4. The Capital Sins: The Roots of Evil

The Church's tradition, drawing on St. John Cassian and St. Gregory the Great, has identified seven principal vices, called «capital» because they are the head (caput) of many other sins. The Catechism lists them in paragraph 1866:

«Vices can be classified according to the virtues they oppose, or also linked to the capital sins which Christian experience has distinguished, following St. John Cassian and St. Gregory the Great. They are called ‘capital’ because they engender other sins, other vices. They are pride, avarice, envy, wrath, lust, gluttony, and sloth or acedia.» (CCC 1866)

Let us examine each one, because knowing the enemy is the first step to defeating it:

1. Pride: The disordered appetite for one's own excellence. It is the original sin of Lucifer («I will not serve») and of Adam («you will be like gods»). Pride is the root of all other sins, because every sin is, at its core, preferring oneself over God. The proud person cannot bear to depend on anyone, does not ask for help, does not acknowledge his own limits. St. Augustine said that humility is the foundation of all virtues because pride is the foundation of all vices.

2. Avarice: The disordered desire to possess material goods. The avaricious person never has enough; his heart is where his treasure is (Mt 6:21), and his treasure is not in heaven. Avarice closes the hands for giving and opens the hands for accumulating. It is the idolatry of money, which St. Paul calls «the root of all evils» (1 Tim 6:10).

3. Envy: Sadness at another's good, perceived as an evil for oneself. The envious person does not want to obtain what the other has; he wants the other not to have it. It is a mean and destructive sin that corrodes the soul and poisons relationships. It was envy that led Cain to kill Abel. As the book of Wisdom writes: «By the envy of the devil, death entered the world» (Wis 2:24).

4. Wrath: The disordered desire for revenge. Not all anger is sin; righteous anger at injustice is a passion that can be virtuous if ordered by reason. But wrath as a capital vice is what loses control, desires the other's harm, and wallows in revenge. It is an explosion that destroys the one who harbors it more than the one at whom it is directed.

5. Lust: The disordered desire for sexual pleasure. Sexuality is a good gift from God, but when it is separated from love, commitment, and openness to life, it becomes lust. This vice reduces the person to an object of pleasure and corrupts the capacity to love. It is especially dangerous in our hypersexualized culture.

6. Gluttony: The disordered appetite for food and drink. It is not a sin to enjoy a good meal; the sin lies in excess, in eating and drinking without measure, in treating the body as an end in itself. Gluttony is the inability to say «enough.» The Desert Fathers taught that gluttony is the first trench that must be conquered, because whoever cannot master his stomach will hardly master more subtle passions.

7. Sloth (acedia): It is not simply physical laziness. In its deep sense, sloth or acedia is sadness at spiritual good, weariness with the things of God, resistance to effort in the life of prayer and virtue. It is the «noonday demon» of which the monks spoke: that temptation to say «this is not worth it,» «I won't pray today,» «after all, it's all the same.» Acedia is the death of the soul by boredom.

The struggle against the capital vices is not a direct struggle (attacking the vice head-on usually reinforces it) but an indirect one: it consists in practicing the contrary virtue. Against pride, humility. Against avarice, generosity. Against envy, charity. Against wrath, meekness. Against lust, chastity. Against gluttony, temperance. Against sloth, spiritual diligence.

5. The Social Dimension of Sin

Sin is not merely an individual act. It has a social dimension that the Catechism underscores and that St. John Paul II developed in depth in his encyclical Sollicitudo Rei Socialis (1987):

«Sin is thus a personal act. Moreover, we have a responsibility for the sins committed by others when we cooperate in them: by participating directly and voluntarily in them; by ordering, advising, praising, or approving them; by not disclosing or not hindering them when we have an obligation to do so; by protecting evildoers.» (CCC 1868)

In this way, personal sins generate structures of sin: unjust economic systems, institutionalized corruption, cultures of death, networks of exploitation. These structures, in turn, push people toward sin, in a vicious circle from which it is hard to escape.

Structures of sin are not abstract entities; they are the result of many personal acts of sin that accumulate and become institutionalized. A company that exploits its workers, a corrupt government that steals from its citizens, an industry that profits from addiction... All of this began with personal decisions that created a system.

But responsibility also works in the opposite direction: we can cooperate in good, build structures of grace, be a leaven of holiness in the world. The Church's social doctrine calls us precisely to this: to transform structures of sin into structures of solidarity.

Sins That Cry to Heaven

The catechetical tradition has identified certain sins as particularly grave because of their social dimension and their cry before God. The Catechism mentions:

«Sins can be distinguished according to their object... or according to the values they attack. The catechetical tradition also recalls that there are ‘sins that cry to heaven’: the blood of Abel, the sin of the Sodomites, the cry of the people oppressed in Egypt, the cry of the foreigner, the widow, and the orphan, injustice to the wage earner.» (CCC 1867)

These sins «cry to heaven» because the injustice they contain is so grave that it seems to cry out for divine intervention. They are sins against the most vulnerable: the murdered innocent (Abel), the oppressed poor, the immigrant, the widow, the orphan, the exploited worker. God hears that cry, as he heard the cry of his people in Egypt (Ex 3:7).

Sins Against the Holy Spirit

Jesus spoke in the Gospel of a sin that «will not be forgiven»: the blasphemy against the Holy Spirit (Mt 12:31; Mk 3:29). The Church's tradition, gathered in the Catechism (CCC 1868), understands this as the deliberate refusal of God's mercy. It is not that God does not want to forgive; it is that the person freely closes himself to receiving forgiveness. He places himself beyond the reach of grace through his own hardening of heart.

Tradition enumerates six forms of this sin: despair of salvation, presumption of being saved without merit, denying the known truth, envy of another's grace, obstinacy in sin, and final impenitence. In all these cases, it is not the magnitude of the sin that prevents forgiveness, but the conscious and voluntary rejection of mercy.

Key Idea: God's mercy is greater than any sin (misericordia Dei maior omni peccato). There is no sin that God cannot forgive if we repent. The only sin that cannot be forgiven is the one that refuses to repent. The measure of divine forgiveness is set by our repentance, not by the gravity of our guilt.

6. Conversion and Penance: It Is Always Possible to Return

We have spoken much about evil. But this lesson does not end here. The Catechism, after setting forth the reality of sin, reminds us of the reality of mercy. And the concrete path to receive it is conversion and penance.

Conversion (metanoia in Greek) is a change of mind and heart. It is not merely «feeling sorry» for what was done; it is a radical turn in the orientation of one's life. It is turning toward God. It is what the prodigal son did when, amid the pigs, he «came to himself» and said: «I will set out and go back to my father's house» (Lk 15:17-18).

Penance is the concrete path of conversion. It includes:

  • Repentance or contrition: sorrow of the soul for having offended God. It can be perfect contrition (sorrow out of love for God) or imperfect contrition or attrition (sorrow out of fear of punishment or the ugliness of sin). Perfect contrition forgives mortal sins if it includes the firm purpose of going to confession.
  • Sacramental confession: the accusation of sins before the priest, who acts in the name of Christ and of the Church. It is the ordinary and secure means of obtaining forgiveness for mortal sins committed after Baptism.
  • Satisfaction or penance: the works the confessor assigns to repair the harm done and restore the broken order. They are not the «price» of forgiveness (Christ already paid the price on the cross) but personal participation in the redemptive work and medicine for the soul.

The Church offers us the sacrament of Penance (Confession) as the Father's embrace of the son who returns. It is not a tribunal of condemnation but a tribunal of mercy. Every confession is a new beginning.

Activity

Read slowly the parable of the prodigal son (Lk 15:11-32). Notice three details: (1) the father sees the son «while he was still a long way off» — he was waiting for him; (2) the father runs to meet him — God does not walk, he runs toward the repentant sinner; (3) the father does not let him finish his apology — he wants no explanations; he wants to restore the relationship.

Question for your notebook: Is there something in your life that distances you from God and for which you need to ask forgiveness? Confession is not for the perfect; it is for the wounded who want to be healed. When was your last Confession?

7. Connection with Scripture

Sacred Scripture is shot through from beginning to end with the reality of sin and the promise of mercy. Let us look at some key texts:

There was a man who had two sons. The younger one said to his father, «Father, give me my share of the estate.» So he divided his property between them. Not long after that, the younger son got together all he had, set off for a distant country and there squandered his wealth in wild living.

After he had spent everything, there was a severe famine in that whole country, and he began to be in need. So he went and hired himself out to a citizen of that country, who sent him to his fields to feed pigs. He longed to fill his stomach with the pods that the pigs were eating, but no one gave him anything.

When he came to his senses, he said, «How many of my father's hired servants have food to spare, and here I am starving to death! I will set out and go back to my father and say to him: Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son; make me like one of your hired servants.»

So he got up and went to his father. But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and was filled with compassion for him; he ran to his son, threw his arms around him and kissed him.

— Luke 15:11-20

The parable of the prodigal son is probably the most perfect image of what sin is and what mercy is. Sin is leaving the Father's house, wasting the inheritance, ending up among pigs. Mercy is the Father who waits, who runs, who embraces, who throws a feast. Note that the son has not finished repenting when the Father has already forgiven him. God's love always goes ahead of our repentance.

And St. Paul sums up the whole economy of salvation in an unforgettable sentence:

«Where sin increased, grace abounded all the more.» (Rom 5:20)

This does not mean that sin is good because it attracts grace. It means that God's grace is so powerful that, even from the worst evil, it can bring about a greater good. The cross is the supreme proof: the greatest sin of humanity (the murder of the Son of God) becomes the source of all grace.

And St. John, with his characteristic clarity, gives us both the diagnosis and the medicine:

«If we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us. If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just, and will forgive our sins and cleanse us from all unrighteousness.» (1 Jn 1:8-9)

Three truths in two verses: (1) we are all sinners — to deny it is to lie to ourselves; (2) confession of sins is the path to forgiveness; (3) God is faithful and just — faithful to his promise of mercy, and just because divine justice consists in restoring what is broken, not in annihilating the guilty.

For Reflection

1. What is your idea of sin: do you see it only as «breaking a rule» or as «breaking a relationship»? What difference does this perspective make?

Think about the difference between a traffic code (a fine for speeding) and a betrayal of a friend. The first breaks an impersonal rule; the second breaks a personal bond. Sin is more the latter. That is why it needs forgiveness, not just correction.

2. Of the seven capital sins, which do you recognize most in your own life? What contrary virtue could you cultivate this week to combat it?

Be honest with yourself. We all deal with one or more of these vices. Identifying it is not condemning yourself; it is beginning the cure. If it is pride: an act of humility. If envy: sincerely rejoicing in another's good. If sloth: extra time in prayer even when you do not feel like it.

3. Do you find it hard to believe that God's mercy is greater than any sin? What experiences or ideas make you doubt this truth?

Sometimes we carry a guilt that prevents us from accepting forgiveness. We think our sin is «too big» to be forgiven. That is inverted pride: thinking that our sin is more powerful than God's mercy. If God forgave David, Peter, and the adulterous woman, why would he not forgive you?

4. Is there any «structure of sin» in your environment (work, school, community) in which you are participating without realizing it?

Complicity with evil is not always active; sometimes it is passive: keeping silent when we should speak, doing nothing when we could help, benefiting from an unjust system. Is there something in your environment that disturbs you and that you could do something about?

5. What holds you back from approaching the sacrament of Confession? If it has been a long time since your last confession, what fear or resistance is in the way?

Shame, fear of the priest's judgment, laziness, thinking «I have no serious sins»... We have all felt some of these resistances. But Confession is not a human judgment; it is God's love waiting for you. As the Curé of Ars said: «It is not the sinner who returns to God to ask forgiveness, but God who runs after the sinner and makes him return to Him.»

Cultural Wealth

Literature St. Augustine, Confessions (397-400 AD), especially Book II (the theft of the pears) and Book VIII (the conversion in the garden in Milan). It is the most influential spiritual autobiography in history, a merciless examination of sin and a hymn to mercy. Many accessible editions are available; the translation by Henry Chadwick (Oxford) and Maria Boulding (New City Press) are particularly recommended.

Literature Fyodor Dostoevsky, Crime and Punishment (1866). The novel is a meditation on sin, guilt, remorse, and redemption. Raskolnikov commits a murder believing he is above the moral law, and the plot follows the process of his conscience until his final conversion, which comes only through Sonia's love.

Literature Victor Hugo, Les Misérables (1862). Jean Valjean, condemned for stealing bread, is transformed by the undeserved forgiveness of Bishop Myriel. The entire rest of the novel is an exploration of how received grace can transform an entire life. It is a modern parable of the prodigal son on an epic scale.

Film The Mission (Roland Joffé, 1986). The character of Mendoza (Robert De Niro), a mercenary turned Jesuit, carries his armor with his past sins as penance. The film explores guilt, penance, forgiveness, and structures of sin (the slave trade, colonial politics). A masterpiece on redemption.

Art Michelangelo, The Last Judgment (1536-1541), Sistine Chapel, Vatican. The monumental fresco depicts Christ the Judge and humanity ascending to heaven or plummeting into hell. Beyond the drama, the painting conveys the radical seriousness of sin and of salvation. The damned are not thrown into hell by a cruel God; it is they who, through their sin, fall by their own weight.

Music Mozart, Requiem, K. 626, especially the Dies Irae and the Lacrimosa. The Dies Irae is a musical meditation on the final judgment and the fear of sin; the Lacrimosa is the plea of the soul that recognizes its guilt and begs for mercy. Mozart composed his own requiem while dying, making it his personal funeral prayer.

Youth Catechism YouCat (Youth Catechism), questions 67-70 (on the reality of sin), 222-229 (on gravity), 314-320 (on penance and conversion). Each question includes a biblical quotation, the CCC text, commentary, and examples for young people. An excellent supplementary resource for this lesson.

Audiovisual Catechesis Real + True (osvnews.com/real-true) has an episode on the capital sins and another on original sin. Ascension Presents with Fr. Mike Schmitz addresses examination of conscience and Confession with clarity and a youthful approach. Also the Bible in a Year and Catechism in a Year podcasts by Fr. Mike Schmitz cover these paragraphs. In Spanish, Infinito + 1 by Juan Manuel Cotelo in his series «Catequizis» dedicates several episodes to sin and mercy.
